title: analysis of biological indicators related to the surface water quality in the danube delta biosphere reserve
author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Lake Furtuna

author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. title: carbon dioxide efflux during the flooding phase of temporary ponds
author: catalan; nuria; von schiller; daniel; marce; rafael; koschorreck; matthias; gomez-gener; lluis; obrador; biel
name: Ponds on Menorca
context: We studied a total of 12 temporary ponds located on the island of Menorca (39°56′N, 4°6′E, Balearic Islands, Western Mediterranean). The climate is Mediterranean, with a dry and hot summer period. The mean monthly temperatures range from 10 °C in January to 25 °C in August. The mean annual precipitation is 549 mm and is typically concentrated in autumn and winter, with November the most humid month and July the driest. The ponds were selected from a detailed inventory of these systems on the island (Fraga et al., 2010) to cover the widest possible spectrum of physicochemical (Pretus et al., 2010), geomorphologic (Cardona et al., 2010) and hydrological properties (Estaún et al., 2010). Thus, the selected ponds range widely in terms of environmental properties (Table 1).

context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Lake Isac

title: carbon dioxide efflux during the flooding phase of temporary ponds
author: catalan; nuria; von schiller; daniel; marce; rafael; koschorreck; matthias; gomez-gener; lluis; obrador; biel
name: Ponds on Menorca
context: We studied a total of 12 temporary ponds located on the island of Menorca (39°56′N, 4°6′E, Balearic Islands, Western Mediterranean). The climate is Mediterranean, with a dry and hot summer period. The mean monthly temperatures range from 10 °C in January to 25 °C in August. The mean annual precipitation is 549 mm and is typically concentrated in autumn and winter, with November the most humid month and July the driest. The ponds were selected from a detailed inventory of these systems on the island (Fraga et al., 2010) to cover the widest possible spectrum of physicochemical (Pretus et al., 2010), geomorphologic (Cardona et al., 2010) and hydrological properties (Estaún et al., 2010). Thus, the selected ponds range widely in terms of environmental properties (Table 1).

title: biogeochemistry of natural ponds in agricultural landscape: lessons learned from modeling a kettle hole in northeast germany
author: onandia; gabriela; lischeid; gunnar; kalettka; thomas; kleeberg; andreas; omari; mohamed; premke; katrin; arhonditsis; george b.
context: Small lentic water bodies (<1 ha) dominate inland waters, both in terms of area and numbers (Downing et al., 2006). Their areal contribution has been underestimated in former times and so has been their role in global cycles (Downing, 2010). Recently, small inland waters such as kettle holes have been increasingly recognized as hotspots of carbon (C) turnover in the landscape (Aufdenkampe et al., 2011; Cole et al., 2007; Premke et al., 2016; Tranvik et al. 2009). Nevertheless, most of the research on C and nutrient cycling is conducted either in large lakes or in shallow lakes or artificial small standing inland waters, whereas the biogeochemistry and ecology of kettle holes have received less attention. Kettle holes are small (< 1 ha) shallow ponds that mostly developed in glacial depressions in extensive areas of North America and Europe when dead ice blocks melted during the retreat of the ice at the end of the last glaciation (Detenbeck et al., 2002; Lischeid and Kalettka, 2012; Tiner, 2003). In Northeast Germany, a region where intensive agriculture prevails, kettle holes are predominant landscape features. They amount to a total number of 150,000 to 300,000 and a density up to \( 40 \ \mathrm{km}^{-2} \), covering as much as 5% of arable land (Kalettka and Rudat, 2006).
name: kettle holes

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
name: small, isolated lakes
context: Our study showed that fish biodiversity was low in these young (<5 years) constructed wetlands. Hansson et al. (2005) also found similar results and indicated that natural wetlands aged more than 100 years showed considerably higher fish species richness than young constructed wetlands. In constructed wetlands, possible sources of the fish are limited to those from nearby streams during floods and mercy releases, and, therefore, the biodiversity of fish is unlikely to increase over a short time period. This study showed that a wetland’s area is also important in determining the species richness of fishes. All treatment cells in our study were <2 ha (Fig. 2). Søndergaard et al. (2005) found that fish species richness was low, typically 1–3 species, in Danish ponds and lakes with areas of <1 ha, and gradually increased with lake size. Scheffer et al. (2006) proposed that fish are rare in small, isolated lakes, probably as a result of a higher likelihood of fish kills combined with their low colonizing ability. Moreover, fish biodiversity appeared to be most affected by the water quality in constructed wetlands. Fish species found in constructed wetlands appeared to be tolerant of high nutrient levels and low DO conditions. Blue gourami is an obligate air-breathing fish (Burggren, 1979), which means that it can adapt to changing DO levels in constructed wetlands. The dominant blue gourami and tilapia in constructed wetlands are exotics, but not the goldfish. Tilapia (Cichlidae) are among the most widely distributed exotic fishes in the world (Canonico et al., 2005). Studies found that they are able to respond to environmental changes by rapidly altering their life history characteristic and trophic levels (Bowen and Allanson, 1982), which may benefit their invasion into new environments (Holway and Suarez, 1999). The exotic species introduced to these wetlands either on purpose or accidentally may

Their areal contribution has been underestimated in former times and so has been their role in global cycles (Downing, 2010). Recently, small inland waters such as kettle holes have been increasingly recognized as hotspots of carbon (C) turnover in the landscape (Aufdenkampe et al., 2011; Cole et al., 2007; Premke et al., 2016; Tranvik et al. 2009). Nevertheless, most of the research on C and nutrient cycling is conducted either in large lakes or in shallow lakes or artificial small standing inland waters, whereas the biogeochemistry and ecology of kettle holes have received less attention. Kettle holes are small (< 1 ha) shallow ponds that mostly developed in glacial depressions in extensive areas of North America and Europe when dead ice blocks melted during the retreat of the ice at the end of the last glaciation (Detenbeck et al., 2002; Lischeid and Kalettka, 2012; Tiner, 2003). In Northeast Germany, a region where intensive agriculture prevails, kettle holes are predominant landscape features. They amount to a total number of 150,000 to 300,000 and a density up to \( 40 \ \mathrm{km}^{-2} \), covering as much as 5% of arable land (Kalettka and Rudat, 2006).
name: kettle holes

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
context: Our study showed that fish biodiversity was low in these young (<5 years) constructed wetlands. Hansson et al. (2005) also found similar results and indicated that natural wetlands aged more than 100 years showed considerably higher fish species richness than young constructed wetlands. In constructed wetlands, possible sources of the fish are limited to those from nearby streams during floods and mercy releases, and, therefore, the biodiversity of fish is unlikely to increase over a short time period. This study showed that a wetland’s area is also important in determining the species richness of fishes. All treatment cells in our study were <2 ha (Fig. 2). Søndergaard et al. (2005) found that fish species richness was low, typically 1–3 species, in Danish ponds and lakes with areas of <1 ha, and gradually increased with lake size. Scheffer et al. (2006) proposed that fish are rare in small, isolated lakes, probably as a result of a higher likelihood of fish kills combined with their low colonizing ability. Moreover, fish biodiversity appeared to be most affected by the water quality in constructed wetlands. Fish species found in constructed wetlands appeared to be tolerant of high nutrient levels and low DO conditions. Blue gourami is an obligate air-breathing fish (Burggren, 1979), which means that it can adapt to changing DO levels in constructed wetlands. The dominant blue gourami and tilapia in constructed wetlands are exotics, but not the goldfish. Tilapia (Cichlidae) are among the most widely distributed exotic fishes in the world (Canonico et al., 2005). Studies found that they are able to respond to environmental changes by rapidly altering their life history characteristic and trophic levels (Bowen and Allanson, 1982), which may benefit their invasion into new environments (Holway and Suarez, 1999). The exotic species introduced to these wetlands either on purpose or accidentally may
name: constructed wetlands

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
name: Danish ponds
context: Our study showed that fish biodiversity was low in these young (<5 years) constructed wetlands. Hansson et al. (2005) also found similar results and indicated that natural wetlands aged more than 100 years showed considerably higher fish species richness than young constructed wetlands. In constructed wetlands, possible sources of the fish are limited to those from nearby streams during floods and mercy releases, and, therefore, the biodiversity of fish is unlikely to increase over a short time period. This study showed that a wetland’s area is also important in determining the species richness of fishes. All treatment cells in our study were <2 ha (Fig. 2). Søndergaard et al. (2005) found that fish species richness was low, typically 1–3 species, in Danish ponds and lakes with areas of <1 ha, and gradually increased with lake size. Scheffer et al. (2006) proposed that fish are rare in small, isolated lakes, probably as a result of a higher likelihood of fish kills combined with their low colonizing ability. Moreover, fish biodiversity appeared to be most affected by the water quality in constructed wetlands. Fish species found in constructed wetlands appeared to be tolerant of high nutrient levels and low DO conditions. Blue gourami is an obligate air-breathing fish (Burggren, 1979), which means that it can adapt to changing DO levels in constructed wetlands. The dominant blue gourami and tilapia in constructed wetlands are exotics, but not the goldfish. Tilapia (Cichlidae) are among the most widely distributed exotic fishes in the world (Canonico et al., 2005). Studies found that they are able to respond to environmental changes by rapidly altering their life history characteristic and trophic levels (Bowen and Allanson, 1982), which may benefit their invasion into new environments (Holway and Suarez, 1999). The exotic species introduced to these wetlands either on purpose or accidentally may

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
name: wetland
context: Species richness, abundance, and \( H' \) of wetland birds increased with wetland area up to about 2 ha (Fig. 3). Species richness of fishes and the taxon richness of aquatic macroinvertebrates increased with wetland area and cover of aquatic macrophytes, respectively. However, relations between environmental variables and other biotic metrics of fish and aquatic macroinvertebrates were not clear.

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
context: Areas of treatment cells varied within and between constructed wetlands. The depositional ponds of the two constructed wetlands were small, at <0.2 ha. Areas of other treatment cells ranged 0.55–0.83 ha in the HS2 and 0.26–1.86 ha in the DN (Fig. 2). The cover of aquatic macrophytes was the highest in heavily vegetated ponds of each constructed wetland, i.e., HS2-2, HS2-4, DN3-A, DN3-B, and DN3-D (Fig. 2).
name: DN3-B

title: analysis of biological indicators related to the surface water quality in the danube delta biosphere reserve
author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Merhei lake

title: agricultural freshwater pond supports diverse and dynamic bacterial and viral populations
author: chopyk et al.
name: temperate freshwater agricultural pond
context: Ten-liter water samples were collected in October 2016, November 2016, and December 2016 from a temperate freshwater agricultural pond in central Maryland, United States (maximum depth of ca. 3.35 meters and a surface area of ca. 0.26 ha). A Honda WX10TA (32 GPM) water pump was used to collect water 15–30 cm below the surface into a sterile polypropylene carboy. Samples were kept in the dark at 4°C and processed within 24 h of collection. In addition, a ProDSS digital sampling system (YSI, Yellow Springs, OH, United States) was used to measure, in triplicate: the water temperature (°C), conductivity (SPC uS/cm), pH, dissolved oxygen (%), oxidation/reduction potential (mV), turbidity (FNU), nitrate (mg/L), and chloride (mg/L).

title: analysis of biological indicators related to the surface water quality in the danube delta biosphere reserve
author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Merhei lake

title: biogeochemistry of natural ponds in agricultural landscape: lessons learned from modeling a kettle hole in northeast germany
author: onandia; gabriela; lischeid; gunnar; kalettka; thomas; kleeberg; andreas; omari; mohamed; premke; katrin; arhonditsis; george b.
context: Small lentic water bodies (<1 ha) dominate inland waters, both in terms of area and numbers (Downing et al., 2006). Their areal contribution has been underestimated in former times and so has been their role in global cycles (Downing, 2010). Recently, small inland waters such as kettle holes have been increasingly recognized as hotspots of carbon (C) turnover in the landscape (Aufdenkampe et al., 2011; Cole et al., 2007; Premke et al., 2016; Tranvik et al. 2009). Nevertheless, most of the research on C and nutrient cycling is conducted either in large lakes or in shallow lakes or artificial small standing inland waters, whereas the biogeochemistry and ecology of kettle holes have received less attention. Kettle holes are small (< 1 ha) shallow ponds that mostly developed in glacial depressions in extensive areas of North America and Europe when dead ice blocks melted during the retreat of the ice at the end of the last glaciation (Detenbeck et al., 2002; Lischeid and Kalettka, 2012; Tiner, 2003). In Northeast Germany, a region where intensive agriculture prevails, kettle holes are predominant landscape features. They amount to a total number of 150,000 to 300,000 and a density up to \( 40 \ \mathrm{km}^{-2} \), covering as much as 5% of arable land (Kalettka and Rudat, 2006).
name: kettle holes

title: agricultural freshwater pond supports diverse and dynamic bacterial and viral populations
author: chopyk et al.
name: temperate freshwater agricultural pond
context: Ten-liter water samples were collected in October 2016, November 2016, and December 2016 from a temperate freshwater agricultural pond in central Maryland, United States (maximum depth of ca. 3.35 meters and a surface area of ca. 0.26 ha). A Honda WX10TA (32 GPM) water pump was used to collect water 15–30 cm below the surface into a sterile polypropylene carboy. Samples were kept in the dark at 4°C and processed within 24 h of collection. In addition, a ProDSS digital sampling system (YSI, Yellow Springs, OH, United States) was used to measure, in triplicate: the water temperature (°C), conductivity (SPC uS/cm), pH, dissolved oxygen (%), oxidation/reduction potential (mV), turbidity (FNU), nitrate (mg/L), and chloride (mg/L).

title: analysis of biological indicators related to the surface water quality in the danube delta biosphere reserve
author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Merhei lake

title: agricultural freshwater pond supports diverse and dynamic bacterial and viral populations
author: chopyk et al.
name: temperate freshwater agricultural pond
context: Ten-liter water samples were collected in October 2016, November 2016, and December 2016 from a temperate freshwater agricultural pond in central Maryland, United States (maximum depth of ca. 3.35 meters and a surface area of ca. 0.26 ha). A Honda WX10TA (32 GPM) water pump was used to collect water 15–30 cm below the surface into a sterile polypropylene carboy. Samples were kept in the dark at 4°C and processed within 24 h of collection. In addition, a ProDSS digital sampling system (YSI, Yellow Springs, OH, United States) was used to measure, in triplicate: the water temperature (°C), conductivity (SPC uS/cm), pH, dissolved oxygen (%), oxidation/reduction potential (mV), turbidity (FNU), nitrate (mg/L), and chloride (mg/L).

title: biodiversity of constructed wetlands for wastewater treatment
author: hsu
name: hemi-marshes
context: Changes in macrophyte composition and cover are among the most important ecological successions in constructed wetlands (Mitsch et al., 2005). Although high macrophyte cover may increase taxa richness and density of aquatic macroinvertebrates, problems can arise as a result of the presence of dense vegetation in wetlands such as consumption of DO and contribution to nutrient loadings as plants decompose (Sartoris et al., 2000). For wastewater treatment, a high cover of aquatic macrophytes was designed as one of the important factors facilitating N retention (Thiere et al., 2011). For wildlife management, a 50:50 ratio of open water to emergent vegetation, i.e., hemi-marshes, was recommended to attract wildlife (Murkin et al., 1997). de Szalay and Resh (2000) indicated that the cover of aquatic macrophytes increased the abundances of some dipteran taxa, which might cause serious nuisance problems in wetlands. Our results also show that taxa responded differently to changes in the cover of aquatic macrophytes (Fig. 6). Since the vegetation has both positive and negative effects on the performance of constructed wetland for wastewater treatment, a proper vegetation balance is needed for optimal treatment performance through vegetation management (Thullen et al., 2005). It is widely accepted that created wetlands, whether for wastewater treatment or not, may never express the full range of ecological variability found in natural wetlands (Brooks et al., 2005). However, as water quality improves due to sequential purification along the treatment cells, the last ponds in a constructed wetland may be capable of supporting biodiversity similar to that of natural wetlands (Jurado et al., 2010).

title: assessment of methane and carbon dioxide emissions in two sub-basins of a small acidic bog lake artificially divided 30 years ago
author: sepulveda-jauregui; armando; martinez-cruz; karla; lau; maximilian; casper; peter
name: small lakes
context: The delivery of fresh dissolved organic carbon (DOC) from the catchment is among the key drivers that have been shown to increase the magnitude of \( \mathrm{CH}_4 \) and \( \mathrm{CO}_2 \) emissions (Drake, Raymond, & Spencer, 2018), especially from small lakes (Chmiel et al., 2016; Gonzalez-Valencia et al., 2014; West, Creamer, & Jones, 2015). However, DOC can comprise different proportions of fresh (ready bio-labile) and more recalcitrant fractions, the latter being older in age, highly aromatic (and thus coloured) and regularly referred to as “humic-like” (Raeke, Lechtenfeld, Wagner, Herzsprung, & Reemtsma, 2016). Small lakes that receive large quantities of recalcitrant DOC generally feature the following: reduced metabolism, lower pH and \( \mathrm{O}_2 \) availability, altered contaminant toxicity, lowered light penetration, longer stratification periods and disfavoured food for fish and benthic communities (Brothers et al., 2014; Hessen & Tranvik, 1998; Wetzel, 2001; Williamson, Morris, Pace, & Olson, 1999). It has been regularly reported that, in small lakes with high DOC load, respiration dominates over primary production, resulting in \( \mathrm{CO}_2 \)

title: biogeochemistry of natural ponds in agricultural landscape: lessons learned from modeling a kettle hole in northeast germany
author: onandia; gabriela; lischeid; gunnar; kalettka; thomas; kleeberg; andreas; omari; mohamed; premke; katrin; arhonditsis; george b.
context: Water temperature, dissolved oxygen (DO) and pH were measured in situ at 0.3 m water depth (WTW CellOx® 325 sensor coupled to a ProfiLine Oxi 3205 and SenTix® 81 pH electrode coupled to a ProfiLine pH 3110 portable meter, respectively). Because water level declined markedly during the study period (Kleeberg et al., 2016a), the distance between the sampling depth (0.3 m) and the sediment decreased accordingly. The water volume dynamics of Rittgarten (Fig. 1, Supplementary Material) were based on water level data measured both manually (with weekly to monthly frequency) but also automatically every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). This information was combined with site-specific bathymetric and topographic data (Kleeberg et al., 2016b). Water temperature was measured every 30 min by a CS451 pressure transducer (Campbell Scientific, USA9). Daily mean photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) was approximated by a sinusoidal function, based on data recorded every 30 min at the CarboZALF experimental area, located \( \approx 6 \) km from Rittgarten. The free-floating macrophyte and submerged macrophyte biomass determined in July 2013 (\( 77 \pm 28 \) g dry weight m\(^{-2}\) and \( 125 \pm 42 \) g dry weight m\(^{-2}\), respectively; Kleeberg et al., 2016a), was assumed to represent the growing season biomass maxima. Similar to Kazanjian et al. (2018) we calculated the biomass seasonal variation by fitting a polynomial curve and considering that biomass was negligible prior to May and after September.
name: Rittgarten

title: aquatic insect assemblages of man-made permanent ponds; buenos aires city; argentina
author: fontanarrosa
context: Sampling sites were located in one of the largest (≈80 ha) green spaces of the city, known as “Parques de Palermo” (Fig 1). This parkland is daily used as a recreational area and frequently is cleaned by the customary collection of garbage and lawn cutting. Four man-made permanent freshwater habitats were selected for the present study. These habitats were classified as ponds according to the following definitions: (a) water bodies between 1 \( m^2 \) and 2 ha in area, which may be permanent or seasonal, including both man-made and natural water bodies (Biggs *et al* 2005) and (b) a water body with a maximum depth of no more than 8 m, offering water plants the possibility to colonise almost the entire area of the pond (Oertli *et al* 2000). The main characteristics of the four studied ponds are summarised in Table 1. Water chemical values recorded on January 2004 were provided by the city authorities. These urban ponds are eutrophic, with differences on their chemical variables. P1, usually shadowing, showed the highest conductivity, chlorides, hardness and the lowest value of dissolved oxygen. Conversely, P4 was the most oxygenated may be due to the great photosynthetic activity, with the highest value of pH, total phosphorus and ammonium. In these ponds, no free-floating plant was observed and the removal of submerged vegetation by the park authorities was observed on each sample date. The surface water temperature of each water body was measured on every sample date with a digital thermometer. The surface areas of water bodies were calculated from aerial photographs provided by the Instituto Geográfico Militar (1998; scale 1:15,000) using GIS-Arc View 3.1.
name: P4

title: assessment of methane and carbon dioxide emissions in two sub-basins of a small acidic bog lake artificially divided 30 years ago
author: sepulveda-jauregui; armando; martinez-cruz; karla; lau; maximilian; casper; peter
name: small lakes
context: The delivery of fresh dissolved organic carbon (DOC) from the catchment is among the key drivers that have been shown to increase the magnitude of \( \mathrm{CH}_4 \) and \( \mathrm{CO}_2 \) emissions (Drake, Raymond, & Spencer, 2018), especially from small lakes (Chmiel et al., 2016; Gonzalez-Valencia et al., 2014; West, Creamer, & Jones, 2015). However, DOC can comprise different proportions of fresh (ready bio-labile) and more recalcitrant fractions, the latter being older in age, highly aromatic (and thus coloured) and regularly referred to as “humic-like” (Raeke, Lechtenfeld, Wagner, Herzsprung, & Reemtsma, 2016). Small lakes that receive large quantities of recalcitrant DOC generally feature the following: reduced metabolism, lower pH and \( \mathrm{O}_2 \) availability, altered contaminant toxicity, lowered light penetration, longer stratification periods and disfavoured food for fish and benthic communities (Brothers et al., 2014; Hessen & Tranvik, 1998; Wetzel, 2001; Williamson, Morris, Pace, & Olson, 1999). It has been regularly reported that, in small lakes with high DOC load, respiration dominates over primary production, resulting in \( \mathrm{CO}_2 \)

title: assessment of methane and carbon dioxide emissions in two sub-basins of a small acidic bog lake artificially divided 30 years ago
author: sepulveda-jauregui; armando; martinez-cruz; karla; lau; maximilian; casper; peter
context: In this study, we assessed the balance between CO\(_2\) and CH\(_4\) accumulated and emitted in two contrasting basins in a long-running whole-lake experiment. In 1986, experimental Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle was first divided into two basins by a plastic curtain (Kasprzak, 1993), and later, in 1989, it was subdivided into four basins (Figure 1). The two western basins receive allochthonous DOC inputs from an adjacent peat bog (Sachse, Babenzien, Ginzel, Gelbrecht, & Steinberg, 2001), keeping the pH at 4.4. Meanwhile, the two eastern basins have remained unaffected by DOC inputs from the adjacent peat bog, favouring a pH increase up to 6.5. Besides this pH increase, biomass and biological activity across all trophic levels in the eastern basins were higher compared to those in the western basins (Hehmann, Krienitz, & Koschel, 2001). Our aim was to evaluate how long-lasting (nearly 30 years) modification of DOC input to a small lake alters its CH\(_4\) and CO\(_2\) dynamics. The experimental whole-lake enclosure allowed the manipulation of nonartificial DOC inputs for almost 30 years, which provided a unique opportunity to assess the effect of DOC reduction on carbon greenhouse gas emission in lakes. Independent from intersystem variations
name: Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle

The experimental whole-lake enclosure allowed the manipulation of nonartificial DOC inputs for almost 30 years, which provided a unique opportunity to assess the effect of DOC reduction on carbon greenhouse gas emission in lakes. name: Lake Grosse Fuchskuhle

title: analysis of biological indicators related to the surface water quality in the danube delta biosphere reserve
author: tudor; m.; tudor; i-m; ibram; o.; teodorof; l.; nastase; c.; deak; g. y.
context: Study area. Lake Isac is situated in a centre of the Danube Delta (45°08′N and 29°17′E), 10.20 km² with a mean depth of 2.4 m. Retention time in the basin is 26 days. Phytoplankton (chlorophyll \( a \)) gross production averaged 130 mg/l during 2009–2013. Rosu is lake situated in the east part of the delta near to the Back sea (45°03′N and 29°35′E) and covers an area of 13.65 km². Average depth is 3.7 m and retention time is 44 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 63.84 mg/l. Merhei lake is situated in the north part of the delta (45°19′N and 29°26′E), 13.68 km² with a mean depth of 1.53 m. Retention time in the basin is 82 days, mean gross production of chlorophyll \( a \) was 66.33 mg/l during 2009–2013. Lake Furtuna is situated in a west of the Danube Delta (45°21′N – 29°12′E), 7.79 km²
name: Lake Isac

title: biogeochemistry of natural ponds in agricultural landscape: lessons learned from modeling a kettle hole in northeast germany
author: onandia; gabriela; lischeid; gunnar; kalettka; thomas; kleeberg; andreas; omari; mohamed; premke; katrin; arhonditsis; george b.
name: Rittgarten
context: The temporal dynamics of the two primary producers and their seasonal succession in the kettle hole were reasonably predicted by the model (Fig. 2). During the study period, DW developed between late spring and early autumn in Rittgarten, peaking in summer, which can be considered as the typical pattern in temperate shallow lakes (Sayer et al., 2010). In lakes where free-floating macrophytes typically prevail, situations of complete absence and dense cover alternate as a function of lake size, morphometry, water level, nutrient concentrations and temperature (O'Farell et al., 2009). The development of dense DW mats in shallow waters has often been linked to a strong decrease in phytoplankton biomass due to severe light attenuation in the water column (Abdel-Tawwab, 2006; Pasztaleniec et al., 2013). Interestingly, there was an extended period (summer to early autumn 2013) in Rittgarten, when relatively high phytoplankton biomass (Chl-\(a\) up to 80 \( \mu \)g L\(^{-1}\)) was found under a dense DW cover. This cover likely favored phytoplankton groups well adapted to low-light availability. Poor-light climate under free-floating macrophyte cover typically triggers changes in the composition of the algal assemblage, with a higher contribution of groups such as cyanophytes, diatoms, cryptophytes, and euglenoids, well adapted to low light conditions (de Tezanos Pinto and O'Farrell, 2014). Similar to Rittgarten observations, intermediate to high Chl-\(a\) concentrations (20–100 \( \mu \)g L\(^{-1}\)) were found in different shallow freshwater systems under dense free-floating macrophyte mats, and this was attributed to differential efficiency for N and P uptake by phytoplankton and floating macrophytes (de Tezanos Pinto and O'Farrell, 2014). Based on the predicted cumulative annual fluxes for nutrient uptake (Fig. 3), we hypothesize that competition for nutrients explains the relatively high phytoplankton biomass in the presence of DW in Rittgarten. Whereas phytoplankton utilized most of the P consumed, duckweed removed most N. The potential of phytoplankton to reduce DW growth by P depletion has been pinpointed by Szabó et al. (1999). Similar to the latter study, we also found that duckweed internal P concentrations in Rittgarten (2 mg P g dry weight\(^{-1}\), Kleeberg et al., 2016a) were below the optimal growth range for DW species they reported (5.7–26.6 mg P g dry weight\(^{-1}\)).
